title: The M. Night Shyamalan Rankings and ‘Old’
format: Sean Fennessey and Van Lathan trace M. Night Shyamalan's whole career, from The Sixth Sense to Old, then rank all twelve movies worst to best.

segments.heading: The Shyamalan Career, Movie by Movie

films.title: The Sixth Sense
films.note: Both hosts had the twist spoiled before seeing it and it didn't ruin the movie. It's the one that put Shyamalan on the cover of Time.

films.title: Unbreakable
films.note: Van's introduction to the film had people kicked out of his house for not liking it; both hosts eventually rank it No. 1.

films.title: Signs
films.note: Mel Gibson at his best; the alien invasion is really about a family working through grief. "Not his best movie, but the one where you go: he's not going to miss."

films.title: The Village
films.note: Shot by Roger Deakins with a stacked cast (William Hurt, Bryce Dallas Howard); the twist is telegraphed in the first ten minutes and both call it a slight letdown at the time.

films.title: Lady in the Water
films.note: Both call it the worst movie of his career. It's the subject of Michael Bamberger's book The Man Who Heard Voices, which chronicles the shoot and Shyamalan's break with Disney.

films.title: The Happening
films.note: A prescient pandemic movie undercut by a Mark Wahlberg performance that, per Wahlberg himself, was based on Shyamalan.

films.title: The Last Airbender
films.note: The one that convinced Van Shyamalan was "completely broken"; he wrote a review at the time declaring the director finished.

films.title: After Earth
films.note: Van's outlier favorite among the low points, tied to his relationship with his late father; he concedes most people don't rate it.

films.title: The Visit
films.note: The low-budget Blumhouse found-footage horror that brought Shyamalan back after the studio-hopping years.

films.title: Split
films.note: James McAvoy's dissociative-identity performance and the closing tie to Unbreakable; Sean liked it less on this rewatch than he remembered.

films.title: Glass
films.note: The trilogy-closer both agree gets lost in its own mythology again: a $20 million movie that made $247 million worldwide.

films.title: Old
films.note: The first 70 to 75 minutes are, per Sean, one of the best thrillers in years before the movie strains under its own construction. He calls it a "solid B."

segments.heading: The M. Night Shyamalan Rankings
segments.note: worst to best, revealed live on the show
segments.subject: M. Night Shyamalan

films.title: Unbreakable
films.note: No. 1, the take they knew would get them "flambéed": better than The Sixth Sense.

films.title: The Sixth Sense
films.note: No. 2: "good, it's dope," but neither host revisits it as often as Unbreakable.

films.title: Signs
films.note: No. 3: the last of the three they consider his best.

films.title: Split
films.note: No. 4: doesn't crack the top three, but clears everything below it easily.

films.title: The Village
films.note: No. 5: edges out The Visit on the strength of its world-building and cast.

films.title: The Visit
films.note: No. 6: the comeback movie, still not quite as impressive to Sean as The Village.

films.title: Old
films.note: No. 7: lands here on the strength of a strong first two-thirds.

films.title: Glass
films.note: No. 8: "sucks, but it's better than The Happening."

films.title: The Happening
films.note: No. 9: "it would be hard for me to recommend any of these four movies to a stranger."

films.title: After Earth
films.note: No. 10: Sean's pick, over Van's objection given his personal attachment to it.

films.title: The Last Airbender
films.note: No. 11: "the nail in the coffin" of his lost period.

films.title: Lady in the Water
films.note: No. 12: the unanimous worst, a "true zero star movie."

referenced.group: Not in the core 12 (Shyamalan's pre-Sixth Sense films)
